coredata.dc:description: The current study focuses on self-assembly of collagen fibrils with Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) and its tissue regenerative applications. GQDs were synthesised by the liquid exfoliation method using ethyl acetoacetate (EAA) as the solvent. The photoluminescence property of the GQD was established by the UV-Vis absorption spectrum. Characteristic increase in thermal stability, enhanced tensile strength was found after the self-assembly of collagen with GQD. The GQDs self-assembled collagen bio-matrices showed 3D surface topology mimicking the extracellular matrix. The GQDs self-assembled collagen bio-matrices did not show toxicity in the structural cells namely fibroblast cells and exhibited increased biocompatibility as indicated by enhanced cell adhesion, proliferation and cell migration of fibroblast cells. Angio-proliferative effect as indicated by an enhanced blood vessel formation and intact unruptured blood vessels was observed in the GQD self-assembled collagen. The study opens a way forward for the use of zero-dimensional graphene based 3-dimensional self-assembled collagen biomatrices for soft tissue regenerative applications.

head.ce:title: Zero dimensional Graphene Quantum Dots self-assembled collagen 3D bio-matrices for soft tissue regeneration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study focuses on self-assembly of collagen fibrils with Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) and its tissue regenerative applications. GQDs were synthesised by the liquid exfoliation method using ethyl acetoacetate (EAA) as the solvent. The photoluminescence property of the GQD was established by the UV-Vis absorption spectrum. Characteristic increase in thermal stability, enhanced tensile strength was found after the self-assembly of collagen with GQD. The GQDs self-assembled collagen bio-matrices showed 3D surface topology mimicking the extracellular matrix. The GQDs self-assembled collagen bio-matrices did not show toxicity in the structural cells namely fibroblast cells and exhibited increased biocompatibility as indicated by enhanced cell adhesion, proliferation and cell migration of fibroblast cells. Angio-proliferative effect as indicated by an enhanced blood vessel formation and intact unruptured blood vessels was observed in the GQD self-assembled collagen. The study opens a way forward for the use of zero-dimensional graphene based 3-dimensional self-assembled collagen biomatrices for soft tissue regenerative applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Collagen is one of the most abundant conserved proteins in mammals. It has being widely used as a biomaterial in the field of tissue engineering applications in the past few decades because of its immense availability. Collagen gives structural integrity and tensile strength to tissues and has been shown to promote tissue regeneration [1–7]. Collagen cross-linking is extremely important to impart mechanical stability to make it in suitable form for biomaterial applications. The cross-linking can be achieved by enzymatic cross-linking using horseradish peroxidase, transglutaminase etc., physical cross-linking using different techniques such as UV irradiation, gamma radiation etc., and chemical cross-linking by use of chemical agents such as formaldehyde, gluteraldehyde, carbodiimide, genipin etc [8,9]. Incorporation of cross-links in collagen fibrils was found to increase the mechanical stability, biocompatibility and less susceptibility to proteolytic degradation.

Advancements in the field of nanotechnology resulted in use of nanotized metal and non- metallic cross linkers of collagen with multifunctional therapeutic properties for tissue regenerative applications [10,11]. Carbon based fluorescent nano-materials namely carbon nano dots (CNDs), Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), carbon nano tubes (CNTs) etc are also gaining attention in biomaterial fabrications [12,13]. Carbon dots are zero dimensional (0D) material with size less than 10 nm. In the recent past carbon based nano-materials have found to attract interest. Carbon dots have been shown to possess excellent biocompatibility antibacterial and wound healing property. Much research on carbon dots is carried out on its applications in optical sensing, bio imaging, drug administration [13]. A number of studies related to the graphene family nanomaterial that is reduced graphene, graphene oxide were reported. However no reports are available in the use of graphene oxide at zero dimensional level for biomaterial applications in particular its role and effect on collagen based biomaterials. In recent years Graphene Quantum Dots are gaining attention when compared to the carbon dots as they possess lesser atomic layer and better crystallanity, show fluorescence and gives higher quantum yield, and large surface area.

Studies have shown that the GQDs have much better application in biological field than any other QDs. Graphene Quantum Dots are the simplest form of carbon-dots which are made by a single layer of carbon core where the chemical groups are connected to its surface or edges [12]. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) have numerous biomedical applications in contrast to graphene or graphene oxide [14,15]. When compared to the metal-based quantum dots (QDs), GQDs have emerged as an alternative because they are composed of basically non-toxic, eco-friendly and bio-friendly [16] element that is carbon, which makes them an appreciably useful and promising bio-analytical tool [14,17–21].

In the present work, Graphene Quantum Dots were synthesized by the liquid exfoliation method by using ethyl acetoacetate as the solvent [22] and the GQDs self-assembled collagen biomatrix was evaluated for its efficacy for tissue regenerative 3 D scaffolds. Till date no bioactivity related to the self-assembly of collagen fibrils using Graphene Quantum Dots was studied. The effect of GQDs on self-assembly of collagen fibrils to form 3 D biomatrix for tissue regenerative applications, its bioactivity, mechanical strength, cell proliferation, angiogenesis were studied in this work. The GQDs induced self-assembly of the collagen was found to have a significant effect in promoting the strength and stability of collagen. The collagen biomatrix self-assembled with GQDs were shown to have superior biological properties in particular they promoted angiogenesis making the biomaterial as a bio-integrative bio matrix.

All the biochemical and chemicals used in this study was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, United States and Hi-Media Laboratories, India, unless otherwise mentioned. Culture grade chemicals were used for all cell culture experiments.

The fibroblast cell line Swiss 3T6 cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium-high glucose with 4500 mg/L glucose, supplemented to contain 3.7 g/L sodium bicarbonate; fetal bovine serum (10 %). This cell lines were procured from NCCS, Pune, Maharashtra, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of collagen from rat tail tendons
ce:section.ce:para: Collagen was being isolated from rat tail tendons by dialysis. The entire procedure was carried out in cold room (4 °C). Rat tail was taken and its outer skin was removed. White fiber like substances (tendons) was pulled out using forceps. This was followed by washing steps. Excess water was removed from the tissues and weight of the tissues was noted. The tendons were then soaked in 0.5 M acetic acid for swelling. The tendons were grinded and centrifuged. Supernatant was collected and purification of collagen was done by salt precipitation method by the addition of 5 % NaCl. The pellet obtained after centrifugation was suspended in 0.5 M acetic acid. Elimination of NaCl was done by dialysing against 0.05 M acetic acid and collagen was stored at 4 °C. Concentration of the collagen were determined and then used for further experiments [3,23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) by liquid exfoliation method
ce:section.ce:para: Ethyl acetoacetate (EAA) was mixed with NaOH with constant stirring for 30 min. The mixture was then filtered with Whatmann filter paper and to this filtrate 200 mg of graphite powder was added [22]. The mixture was then sonicated for 2 hrs. It was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to eliminate the unexfoliated graphite and larger GQDs which were then filtered using a syringe filter (0.22 µM). The filtrate was then kept for dialysis by using a dialysis membrane with cut-off of 2 kDa for 24 hrs. The yellow coloured solutions outside the dialysis membrane were taken and then freeze dried (lyophilisation). The resultant GQDs paste was then dispersed in water as GQDs solution and was used for further experiments.

The quality of the as-synthesised GQD was studied by the Raman spectroscopy. The synthesised GQDs by the liquid exfoliation method were dispersed in water and were analysed using Confocal Micro Raman Spectrometer instrument [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM with EDAX of Graphene Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, shape and elemental analysis of the Graphene Quantum Dots were analysed using TESCAN CLARA Ultra-High Resolution SEM instrument equipped with Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDAX) spectrophotometer [25,26]. The GQDs were spread on a glass slide and was sputter coated with gold for 2–3 min on the stub which was analysed in a high vacuum where a strong beam of electrons are passed onto the sample and images were obtained accordingly.

Transmission Electron microscopic images [12] of the Graphene Quantum Dots were acquired using a Jeol/JEM 2100 HR-TEM instrument. The size and morphology of the synthesised GQDs were further confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembly of collagen fibrils by GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Fibrillogenesis or self-assembly of collagen fibrils was carried out by mixing 600 µg/mL concentration of collagen with 0.2 M PBS and 2 M sodium chloride and the pH was adjusted to 7.0–7.4 by using 1.25 N sodium hydroxide [27,28]. GQD of 100 µg/mL to 500 µg/mL was added concurrently along with the collagen and the turbidity was measured at 313 nm using JASCO V770 UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The t1/2 i.e., the rate of fibril formation was measured by calculating the time taken to reach half the value of the final turbidity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of collagen biomatrix self-assembled with GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Collagen was reconstituted by adjusting the pH to 7.0–7.5 which was kept as control. GQD induced self-assembled collagen biomatrix was prepared simultaneously by adding GQD so that the final concentration of GQDs was 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL respectively. The solutions were allowed to gel at 37 °C. The collagen gels were freeze dried (lyophilisation) completely. The lyophilised collagen bio-matrix was stored until used for further experiments. Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images of the collagen scaffold self-assembled with GQD were analysed. The sheets were sputter coated with gold for 2–3 min on stubs and images were obtained with TESCAN CLARA Ultra-High Resolution (UHR) SEM instrument.

The tensile strength of the control collagen and collagen self-assembled with GQDs were done using Universal Testing Machine (UTM) [8,22,29]. 5 × 5 cm of collagen sheets were prepared and used for the analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of collagen biomatrix self-assembled with GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability analysis of native and GQD self-assembled with collagen was done by a TGA analysis using TGA Q50 V20.5 Build 30 instrument [3,22,29]. The samples were exposed to a constant heating rate of 20 °C/min at temperature ranging from room temperature to 800 °C. The reduction in weight of the sample (in percentage) with an increase in temperature was recorded and graph was being plotted.

Thermal stability of the native and GQD self-assembled with collagen was done by Differential Scanning Colorimeter. The samples were exposed to a constant heating rate at 10 °C/min at temperature ranging from room temperature to 300 °C.

To study the potential cytotoxicity of different concentrations of GQDs, 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was carried out which measures the metabolic activity of cells [30]. 48 well plates were used which was coated with native collagen as control and collagen self-assembled with different concentrations of GQDs (100–500 µg/mL). When the cells attained 80–90 % confluency, it was harvested using trypsin-EDTA solution and approximately, 12,000 cells per well were seeded and left overnight in a CO2 incubator 37 °C temperature and 5 % CO2. After 24 h, the culture medium was removed and 0.5 mg/mL MTT solution was added and incubated in dark at 37 °C. After 4 h of incubation, MTT solution was removed and the purple insoluble formazan crystals were solubilized using dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). The optical density was read at 570 nm using Bio-Rad ELISA plate reader equipment, USA. The cell viability was calculated from the equation:

48 well plates were taken and coated with control collagen and collagen self-assembled with GQDs (400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL). Approximately 10,000–11,000 cells/well were seeded. Cell proliferation was studied by performing MTT assay for 24, 48 and 72 hrs respectively.

The cell viability of the Swiss 3T6 cell line on the collagen self-assembled with GQD were analysed by the double staining technique i.e., AO/PI staining [31]. Collagen self-assembled with GQDs (400 µg/mL, 500 µg/mL) and control collagen were reconstituted in a 24 well plate and allowed to dry completely. About 30,000–35,000 cells/well were seeded and allowed to attach and grow over the bio-matrices. After 24 hrs the media was discarded and Acridine orange (2 µM) was added to the cells and incubated for about 10 min at 37 °C. The cells were washed with PBS to wash off the unreacted Acridine orange and then treated with Propidium iodide (4 µM). The fluorescent images of the cells were taken by using Leica DMi8 microscope.

Crystal violet assay was done to determine the adhesion efficacy of cells collagen biomatrix self-assembled with GQDs. 0.1 % crystal violet was prepared in 20 % ethanol. Control collagen and collagen self-assembled with GQDs of different concentration (400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL) were reconstituted in a 48 well plate and allowed to dry completely. About 20,000–25,000 cells/well were seeded on to these bio-matrices. The cells were fixed with chilled methanol and incubated for 10–15 min. The cells were then treated with 0.1 % crystal violet and incubated for about 10–15 min. The cells were then gently washed with double distilled water in order to remove the excess stain. The stains retained in the cells were then solubilised by adding 500 µL of methanol. The optical density was read at 560 nm using Bio-Rad ELISA plate reader and graph was plotted accordingly.

Plasma was removed and RBCs from rat blood were washed 2–3 times with 150 mM NaCl by centrifugation [32]. Discard the supernatant and washed the RBCs with 1X PBS (pH- 7.4). Discard supernatant and suspend the RBCs in PBS. The RBCs was diluted 1:50 with PBS. 400 µL of diluted RBCs were incubated with control collagen and the collagen self-assembled with GQD sheets at 37 °C for 1 h. The RBC solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. 100 µL of supernatant was transferred in a 96 well plate, and the optical density of the supernatant was read at 540 nm. 20 % Triton X-100 was used as positive control and PBS (pH-7.4) was used as the negative control respectively.

Cell migration was analysed by the scratch wound assay also known as in vitro wound healing assay [33]. The effect of Graphene Quantum Dots self-assembled collagen on the Swiss3T6 fibroblast cell’s proliferation and migration were analysed using scratch wound assay. Control collagen and collagen self-assembled with GQDs of different concentrations (400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL) were reconstituted and coated onto a 24 well plate. About 80,000 cells/well were trypsinized and seeded in the wells and allowed to attach. After attaining confluency a scratch was mechanically made in the centre of the wells perpendicularly. The detached cells were washed off with PBS after scratching. To this fresh DMEM high glucose medium without FBS was added and kept in incubator at 37 °C and 5 % carbon dioxide. Photos of the cell migration at 24 hrs were taken. The percentage of wound closure was measured by the following equation:
Wound

3rd day fertilized egg was being obtained from the Tamilnadu egg hatchery, Potheri, Chennai. The egg was then taken and cracked open carefully at the blunt end of the egg with a sharp-edged forceps and white inner membrane inside the egg protecting the embryo was removed carefully without disturbing or damaging the embryo and the blood vessels around it [34]. The sample, that is the control collagen and the collagen self-assembled with 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL GQDs sheets were cut into a small piece and kept over the CAM (Chorioallantoic Membrane) that is the junction of the blood vessels where the major blood vessels bifurcated with the help of forceps carefully under asceptic condition. The egg was then sealed with parafilm and kept for 48 hrs in incubator. The image of the embryo along with the samples was taken at different time intervals. The angiogenesis in the chick embryo was studied and the effect of the GQD on them was evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Graphene Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene QDs were synthesised by the liquid exfoliation method where ethyl acetoacetate was used as the solvent. NaOH at a concentration of 1.67 mg/mL was used here as it results in the higher exfoliation yield and also expansion of the interplanar spaces in the graphite layers thus resulting in the easier exfoliation when sonicating [22].

The yield obtained after 2 hrs of sonication followed by dialysis and lyophilisation was found to be 1.628 g/mL. The fluorescence property of the resultant GQD synthesised by this method was captured under UV-transilluminator [ Fig. 1. (A) and (B)]. The excitation and emission spectrum of GQDs were studied by the UV spectrophotometer [35–38]. As shown, in Fig. 1. (C) the UV-Vis absorption spectrum shows the transition absorption peak around 240 nm (π-π * transition), the transition absorption peak around 378 nm (n-π * transition), and a long tail that is extended to the visible region. The carbon materials also have properties of showing excitation-dependent and independent photoluminescence [Fig. 1. (D)]. The fluorescence spectrum of the GQDs showed peak 485 nm. The photoluminescence peak observed in the photoluminescence spectra around 485 nm might be due to the effect of intrinsic and defect emission. The photoluminescence mechanism explains the quantum size effect, edge effect and defect effect.

Raman spectroscopy analysis of as-synthesised GQDs was also studied with excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Raman spectroscopy is a versatile method for studying the properties of graphene QDs. Fig. 1. (E) shows the confocal micro Raman spectrophotometer. The spectra show the typical graphitic features of the graphene where it mainly shows two fundamentally different sets of peaks, that is the D band which is indicated as the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite and G bands are found to be associated with the vibration of the sp2 bonded carbon atoms in the 2D hexagonal lattice. The D band was found to be around 1350 cm−1 and the G band was found to be around 1588 cm−1 respectively [39,40]. The Raman spectra confirmed the formation defects in GQDs as observed by a broadened D band in Raman spectra which indicates the quantum properties of the GQDs. The ratio of intensities of the disordered D and crystalline G bands (D/G) was found to be 0.85. The ratio indicated that they are found to be highly crystalline. The zeta potential analysis of the GQDs showed a potential of − 27.6 mV and confirms that the GQDs possess good stability (Supplementary Fig. 1). Further the stability of the GQDs in PBS were analysed by measuring the UV Vis peak after storage in PBS for 120days. It was observed that GQDs showed peak around 235 nm indicating that no aggregation of the GQDs occurred even after 120 days of storage (Supplementary Fig. 2A). FTIR analysis was performed to determine the presence of chief functional groups in GQDs. We observed peaks at 3327 cm-1 and 2158 cm-1 corresponds to the symmetric O-H stretching vibration and stretching of C-H respectively. The peak at 1747 cm-1 shows the stretching of CO. The absorption spectra at 1625 cm-1 attributes to the stretching vibration of CC while the peak at 1359 cm-1 corresponds to CH stretching. The band at 1204 cm-1 shows the presence of C-O. The peak patterns observed were typical for the GQDs indicating the formation of GQDs. The peaks were recorded from 400 to 4000 cm-1 under transmittance mode with a scanning speed of 2 mm/sec using FTIR-4700 type A. (Supplementary Fig. 2B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM with EDAX and TEM of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and shape of the as-synthesised GQDs were examined by the Scanning Electron microscope and EDAX spectra to determine its elemental composition and topology. Fig. 2. (A) shows the SEM images of Graphene Quantum Dots taken at 2 different magnifications and Fig. 2. (B) shows the EDAX spectra elemental analysis of the GQDs.

The SEM image shows that GQDs are distributed uniformly and are found to be aggregated having a spherical shape. The presence of carbon (50.21 %) and oxygen (49.79 %) in the EDAX spectrum indicates the successful formation of Graphene Quantum Dots. Fig. 2. (C) shows the TEM images of GQDs. The HR TEM image of GQDs showed clear lattice fringes which confirm its crystalline nature. The Inter planar spacing (Lattice spacing) of GQDs was observed to be around 0.23 nm. The sizes of the GQDs formed were observed to be in the range of 2 nm.

Fibrillogenesis is a self- assembly process where the collagen forms fibrils spontaneously in-vitro and is found to be an important intermediary when preparing a biomaterial. The fibril formation ability of the GQD self-assembled collagen has being studied by the fibrillogenesis assay. Fig. 3 shows the fibrillogenesis turbidity curve of control and GQD self-assembled collagen of 100 µg/mL to 500 µg/mL concentrations respectively measured at 25 °C (absorbance of 313 nm). The rate of fibril formation is represented as t1/2, which is the turbidity half at log phase of fibrillation curve. t1/2 values is obtained by plotting optical density against time in minutes. The t1/2 values can indicate the efficacy of various molecules in promoting the collagen fibril formation. The lower the t1/2 values the higher the efficacy of the molecules to induce fibrillation of collagen. The control collagen showed a t1/2 of 4.03 min and 100–300 µg/mL concentrations of self-assembled collagen with GQDs showed t1/2 of 3.74 min 400 µg/mL concentration of self-assembled collagen with GQDs showed t1/2 of 3.46 min whereas 500 µg/mL concentration of self-assembled collagen with GQDs showed t1/2 of 3.17 min. The result indicated that the self-assembly of collagen with GQDs increased the rate of fibril formation in concentration dependent manner when compared with the control native collagen. The GQD provides nucleation sites that makes the collagen fibril align and self assembles to form collagen fibrils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Topology of Graphene Quantum Dots self-assembled collagen Biomatrix
ce:section.ce:para: SEM analysis was performed to understand the structural topography of the collagen bio matrices prepared by cross-linking GQD. Fig. 4 shows the Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) [41,42] images of the control collagen and collagen self-assembled with GQD. A significant three-dimensional porous structural topography with network like structure resembling in vivo ECM was observed in collagen self-assembled with GQD (400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL). The furrowed 3D patterning in a jumbled and arbitrary fashion was observed in GQD self-assembled collagen scaffolds in contrast to the control collagen where no such random arrangement was formed which suggested that the self-assembly with GQDs was found to have an effect in the morphology. The native collagen scaffold showed sheet like morphology. The 3D morphology of the GQDs self-assembled bio-matrix would provide sites for the cells to attach, spread and proliferate when compared to the native collagen bio-matrix.

Tensile strength of collagen bio-matrices were analysed by the Universal Test Machine. Mechanical strength of the control collagen and the collagen self-assembled with GQDs were carried out in order to check the mechanical strength of these materials. The tensile strength, elongation at break and Young’s modulus were analysed for these bio-matrices. Table 1 shows the tensile strength, elongation at break and the Young’s modulus of the collagen bio-matrices.

The tensile strength of a material is determined by the Young’s modulus which related to stress by strain. It indicates the capacity of a material to withstand breakage when a particular force is being applied to it. The GQDs self-assembled collagen showed better tensile strength than the native collagen. The Young’s modulus was found to be 770.914 MPa and 778.583 MPa for 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL GQD self-assembled collagen respectively which was found to be greater than the Young’s Modulus of control collagen (561.167 MPa). The result showed an increase in Young’s Modulus of the collagen scaffolds self-assembled with GQDs when compared to the native collagen bio-matrix. When the concentration of GQD was increased, there was an increase in Young’s Modulus of collagen bio matrices. The results thereby indicated that the GQD provides better cross-linking of collagen and increase its tensile strength when compared to the native collagen control. The Young’s modulus of the biomaterials is very important parameter that determines their suitability for their use in tissue regenerative application. The Young's modulus of human skin is about 5-200 MPa. Hence, greater the Young’s modulus better its suitability for application in tissue regeneration. In our work we observed that collagen self-assembled with GQDowed young’s modulus of around 770 MPa when compared to native collagen with young’s modulus of 560 MPa.

The thermal stability of collagen biomatrices was analysed by the Differential Scanning Colorimetry (DSC) and the Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) [43,44]. Fig. 5. (A) shows the Thermogravimetric Analysis of the control and the collagen self-assembled with different concentrations of GQDs. The thermal degradation of the native collagen and collagen self-assembled with the GQDs was observed to occur in 3 stages [4]. First stage or the initial stage is the removal of absorbed water molecule from the collagen matrix, and it is found to be 100 °C in control collagen and 122 °C and 151 °C respectively for the 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL GQDs self-assembled collagen biomatrix. The next stage is the removal of structural water where the transition in temperature occurs at 245 °C in case of control collagen and 281 °C and 262 °C for the 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL GQD self-assembled collagen respectively.

The last stage is the partial breakdown of intermolecular bonding and the complete thermal decomposition which was observed at a temperature of 416 °C in case of control collagen and 435 °C and 460 °C in case of the collagen self-assembled with GQDs respectively. The percentage residues remaining was found to be 22 % in case of control collagen, 29 % for 400 µg/mL GQD self-assembled collagen and 33 % for 500 µg/mL GQD self-assembled with collagen at 800 °C. The reduction in the weight loss was found to be due to the crosslinking of GQDs to the collagen fibril. The percentage residues remaining were found to increase with increase in concentration of GQDs used for self-assembly of collagen fibrils. The result showed an increase in thermal stability for collagen sheet self-assembled with GQDs when compared to collagen control.

The absorption and reduction in the energy of a sample while heating or cooling is studied by the Differential Scanning Colorimetry. The increased hydrothermal stability of the material can be analysed by DSC analysis. The collagen after crosslinking exhibits low denaturation rate and high melting temperature which increases the stability of collagen molecule. Fig. 5. (B) shows the Differential Scanning Colorimetry graph of control collagen and collagen self-assembled with different concentrations of GQDs. The graph shows that the self-assembly of collagen with GQDs helped in increasing the denaturation time and temperature of the collagen when comparined to the control collagen. The control collagen was found to get denatured at 117.53 °C. The collagen on self-assembly with 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL GQDs showed a denaturation temperature of 187.65 °C and 194.79 °C respectively. The water holding capacity of the collagen increases when it is gets self-assembled with GQDs which result in requirement of more heat energy for breaking of these bonds for undergoing denaturation. The result indicates that the crosslinking of collagen with GQDs brought the collagen fibrils in close proximity which resulted in a higher denaturation temperature and increased stability of collagen to denaturation.

The cytotoxicity of a biomaterial is very important for its application especially as a bio-matrix for soft tissue regeneration. A biomaterial should be non-toxic in nature in order for the cells to grow without any inhibition [41,45]. The cytotoxicity of the GQDs was analysed using the Swiss 3T6 fibroblast cell lines.

The cell viability of collagen self-assembled with the Graphene Quantum Dots was compared with the collagen control. The results of the MTT assay showed that there was no significant toxicity and no evident toxic effect on the viability of the cells. The cells maintained on collagen biomatrix self-assembled with GQDs even at concentrations as higher as 500 µg/mL showed 90 % viability [ Fig. 6. (A)]. The result indicated that the cells showed exceptional biocompatibility, even at higher concentrations of GQDs. The cells morphology were observed under the phase contrast microscope (Leica) [Fig. 6. (B) and Fig. 6. (C)], and found that the cells superlatively maintained good morphology and spreading irrespective of the concentrations of GQDs used for crosslinking of collagen in this study. The cell proliferation study even till 72 hrs showed no significant toxic effect on the viability of the cells [Fig. 6 (D)]. The result confirmed that the as-synthesised biomaterial incorporated with GQDs does not induce any cytotoxic behaviour.

AO/PI staining was carried out in Swiss 3T6 fibroblast cells maintained on native collagen (control) and collagen self-assembled with 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL concentrations of GQDs. The AO/PI staining assay was carried out to substantiate the results observed with cell viability assay. Fig. 7. (A) shows the fluorescence microscopic images of cells maintained on native collagen (control) and collagen self-assembled with 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL concentrations of GQDs. The fluorescent images showed that the cells exhibited green fluorescence [3,46] indicating that the bio-matrices did not induced any cytotoxicity. Collagen self-assembled with GQDs (400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL) did not show any cell death. The result further confirmed that even at higher concentrations of the GQDs the cell growth and viability was not affected and cross-linking of collagen with GQDs does not impart any toxicity to the cells. The results of the AO/PI staining are consistent with the results of the MTT assay. The results suggested that collagen self-assembled with GQDs provide a suitable environment for the cells to grow and proliferate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell adhesion and spreading on GQD self-assembled collagen biomatrix
ce:section.ce:para: Cell adhesion is the process in which the cells attach and spreads on to the surface of the biomaterial. The ability of the cells to adhere to a particular surface can be measured calorimetrically by the crystal violet assay [47]. The viable cells adhered on the biomatrix surface take up the crystal violet stain, which binds to the DNA and proteins. The dead cells do not take up the stain as they get detached from the surface and would be washed off. From this staining method the viability and adherence of the cells to a particular substrate and its toxic effects on the cells can be determined effectively. Fig. 7. (B) (i) shows the percentage cell adhesion on collagen and collagen self-assembled with GQDs bio-matrices [Fig. 7. (B) (ii)]. The results showed that the cells readily adhere within 1–2 hrs onto the surface of collagen scaffolds self-assembled with GQDs. The percentage adherence of the fibroblast cells were found to be more than 10–20 % higher on collagen scaffolds self-assembled with GQDs in comparison with the control collagen scaffolds. The result indicated that with the increase in the concentration of GQDs significant increase in the adhesion of cells were observed. About 20 % more cells were adhered to the surface of the collagen self-assembled with 500 µg/mL GQDs compared to control. The result indicated that the GQDs self-assembled collagen scaffolds exhibited the capacity to provide a toxic free environment for the cells to attach and proliferate. The result further showed that the scaffolds effectively had properties which help in the rapid adhesion of the cells to the surface of the material at a short period of time indicating the effective cell adhesion property of the GQD self-assembled collagen biomaterial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hemocompatibility of GQD self-assembled collagen biomatrix
ce:section.ce:para: When considering biomaterials for tissue engineering applications, hemocompatibility is considered to be a key factor since the desired biomaterial remains in close contact to the blood in a wounded area [48]. Substances that show less than 5 % hemolysis are said to be hemocompatible. Hemocompatibility of GQD self-assembled with collagen was studied and compared with native collagen scaffolds. Here Triton-x-100 was used as the positive control and RBCs suspended in PBS was used as the negative control. The results of this assay showed good hemocompatibility in collagen self-assembled with GQDs with only a negligible the percentage 0.3 % hemolysis as shown in Table 2. Fig. 8 shows the photograph of the suspended RBCs after centrifugation on treatment with different collagen scaffolds self-assembled with various concentrations of GQDs.

The percentage hemolysis of collagen self-assembled twithGQD (400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL) was found to be 0.340 % and 0.379 % respectively. The value was found to be less than 5 % which confirms the hemocompatible nature of the collagen self-assembled with GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GQD self-assembled collagen biomatrix on In-vitro scratch wound healing and Cell migration
ce:section.ce:para: Cell migration is a very important step for the restoration of the normal structure of the skin in a wounded area. In-vitro wound healing assay [49–51] is a simple, effective and economical way to study the cell migration in-vitro. For an appropriate wound healing to occur, fibroblast cells needs to migrate to the wounded area which is found to be an essential feature for tissue regeneration in a wounded area. Swiss 3T6 fibroblast cells were used here to study the wound healing capacity of GQD self-assembled collagen biomatrix. Fig. 9. (A) shows the in-vitro scratch wound assay. A prodigious difference was found in wound closure within 24hrs when comparing to the wound area created at 0th hr in GQDs self-assembled collagen bio-matrices. It was found that a faster consequential cell migration and wound closure was observed after 24hrs in collagen self-assembled with GQDs (400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL) than the control collagen. Fig. 9. (B) shows the graphical representation of the percentage of wound area recovered after 24 hrs.

It was observed that 80 % and 92 % wound area closure occured in wounds created in cells maintained on 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL GQDs collagen scaffolds respectively. The cells maintained on control collagen showed only 60 % wound closure after 24 hrs. The wound closure rate was also found to be increase with increase in concentration of GQD used for the self-assembly of collagen fibrils. The result showed that GQDs self-assembled collagen accelerated the rate of wound closure within 24 hrs and can be exclusively used as biomaterial for wound healing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Angio-inductive property of GQD self-assembled Collagen Biomatrix
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity and the angiogenic activity (to induce blood vessel formation) of the GQD collagen biomatrix were investigated in in-ovo Chorioallantoic membrane assay [52–55]. Fig. 10. A shows the images of CAM at different time intervals (0–48 hrs) treated with control collagen and collagen self-assembled with GQDs and Fig. 10. B shows the graphical representation of the number of newly formed blood vessels at different time intervals. No rupture of blood vessels was found in CAMsndicating that no toxicity was induced in CAMs treated with 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL concentrations of GQDs self-assembled collagen scaffolds and control collagen scaffolds. It was noted that more blood vessel formation was observed in collagen self-assembled with 400 µg/mL and 500 µg/mL GQDs. The thickness of blood vessel was also increased subsequently in 48 hrs. Angiogenesis is an important factor in biomaterial integration. Biomaterials should possess angio proliferative properties in order to avoid probabilities of rejection from the host tissues and hence its should provide compatibility in the growth and proliferation of new blood vessels (vascularization) [56,57]. The result indicated that the GQD self-assembled collagen possesses more compatibility and was able to induce angiogenesis effectively.

In this work we have summarized the effect of GQD on self-assembly of collagen fibrils to form bio scaffolds for tissue regenerative activity. GQD apart from aiding in self-assembly of collagen fibrils added-in therapeutic properties appropriate as a 3D biomatrix for tissue engineering applications. GQD effectively self-assembled collagen fibrils by cross-linking the fibrils still retaining the essential properties namely biocompatibility, cell proliferation, increased mechanical stability and bio-integration etc., which play important role in soft tissue regenerations. The in vitro wound closure, cellular migration and in vivo angiogenesis induction was found to be significantly faster in GQDs self-assembled collagen bio-matrices. The study opens new directions in the development of a Graphene Quantum Dot self-assembled collagen which was found to be better biomaterial for soft tissue engineering applications.
